learning.summary: FAT32 is a widely supported file system that has been used across many operating systems for decades. Its broad compatibility makes it useful for removable media, but it has important file and volume size limitations.
learning.explanation:
FAT32 is part of the File Allocation Table (FAT) family of file systems and is recognized for its compatibility across many operating systems and devices. This broad support has made it a common choice for USB flash drives, memory cards, and other removable storage.

Although FAT32 is highly compatible, it has notable limitations. It supports partition (volume) sizes up to approximately 2 TB and has a maximum individual file size of 4 GB. These limits can make it unsuitable for storing very large files.

Modern file systems such as NTFS and exFAT address many of FAT32's limitations by providing additional features or larger file-size support.

facts.text: FAT32 is supported by many different operating systems.
FAT32 supports volumes up to approximately 2 TB.
The maximum file size in FAT32 is 4 GB.
FAT32 is part of the File Allocation Table (FAT) family.

examples.text: A USB flash drive is formatted with FAT32 so it can be read by many different operating systems and devices.

examTips: Memorize the 4 GB maximum file size and approximately 2 TB volume limit.
commonMistakes: Confusing the FAT32 file-size limit with the maximum partition size.
scenarios: Select a file system for a removable drive that must work across multiple operating systems.
pbqIdeas: Choose the appropriate file system based on compatibility and file-size requirements.

reviewNotes: General removable-media example added as illustrative enrichment beyond the transcript evidence.
